result:
All 5 hits in eloop_win.c are also real code. No hits appear inside string literals or comments — grep's 10 total matches LSP(5) + manual verification(5) = 10.

## Result

**10 real code references to `eloop_remove_timeout()`** across two separate implementations of the eventloop, in `src/utils/`. None are inside strings or comments.

**`src/utils/eloop.c`** (POSIX/Linux implementation — in `compile_commands.json`, confirmed via `LSP.findReferences` at the definition, 5/5 matched grep exactly):
- Line 652 — `static void eloop_remove_timeout(...)` — the definition
- Line 674 — call inside `eloop_cancel_timeout()`
- Line 702 — call inside `eloop_cancel_timeout_one()`
- Line 1008 — call inside `eloop_run()`'s dispatch loop
- Line 1084 — call inside `eloop_destroy()`'s cleanup loop

**`src/utils/eloop_win.c`** (Windows implementation — **not** in `compile_commands.json`, so invisible to clangd/LSP; verified manually by reading the file):
- Line 285 — `static void eloop_remove_timeout(...)` — the definition (separate static function, same name, different TU)
- Line 305 — call inside `eloop_cancel_timeout()`
- Line 333 — call inside `eloop_cancel_timeout_one()`
- Line 594 — call inside `eloop_run()`'s dispatch loop
- Line 656 — call inside `eloop_destroy()`'s cleanup loop

**Method/coverage note:** `eloop_remove_timeout` is `static`, so it's two independent, per-file symbols rather than one function called from two places — `eloop.c` is the POSIX event loop backend, `eloop_win.c` the Windows one, and only one is active per build. Only `eloop.c` is part of the active build config, so LSP's `findReferences` only covers it; `eloop_win.c`'s 5 occurrences were confirmed by direct file reads since it's outside the compile DB. Grep total (10) matches the combined LSP+manual count (5+5), so nothing is missing and there are no string/comment false positives to exclude.
